name: History
description: World events, wars, leaders, and historical milestones

questions.q: In what year did the United States declare independence from Great Britain?

source.notes: Declaration adopted July 4, 1776.

questions.q: Who served as Prime Minister of the United Kingdom during most of World War II?

source.notes: PM from May 1940 to July 1945.

questions.q: What was the last major battle of the American Revolutionary War?

source.notes: October 1781. Cornwallis surrendered to Washington.

questions.q: Who was the principal author of the Declaration of Independence?

source.notes: Drafted in June 1776 at age 33.

questions.q: Who was the first person to sign the Declaration of Independence, famously in large handwriting?

source.notes: President of the Continental Congress. His name became slang for 'signature.'

questions.q: Which U.S. president issued the Emancipation Proclamation in 1863?

source.notes: Declared slaves in Confederate states 'forever free.' Took effect Jan 1, 1863.

questions.q: Which Confederate general commanded the Army of Northern Virginia during the U.S. Civil War?

source.notes: Surrendered to Grant at Appomattox Court House, April 9, 1865.

questions.q: In which U.S. state did Rosa Parks refuse to give up her bus seat in 1955?

source.notes: Montgomery, Alabama. Sparked the Montgomery Bus Boycott.

questions.q: President John F. Kennedy launched which volunteer program to send Americans to help developing countries?

source.notes: Established by executive order March 1, 1961.

questions.q: The Watergate scandal, which led to a presidential resignation, involved a break-in at an office building in which city?

source.notes: Break-in at DNC headquarters, June 17, 1972. Nixon resigned August 9, 1974.

questions.q: Explorer Vasco da Gama, who found a sea route to India, came from which country?

source.notes: Reached India in 1498 via the Cape of Good Hope.

questions.q: The Roman military formation 'testudo' (meaning 'tortoise') involved soldiers doing what?
choices: Locking shields overhead and in front

source.notes: Protected against arrows and projectiles during sieges.

questions.q: Before it was renamed in 1847, San Francisco was known by what Spanish name?

source.notes: Yerba Buena means 'good herb' in Spanish.

questions.q: Which 11th-century Scottish king became the subject of a famous Shakespeare tragedy?

source.notes: Ruled Scotland ~1040-1057. Real Macbeth was quite different from Shakespeare's version.

questions.q: The Tiananmen Square protests of 1989 took place in which country?

source.notes: Pro-democracy demonstrations, April-June 1989. Beijing.

questions.q: In 1588, which country sent a massive naval fleet called the Armada to invade England?

source.notes: 130 ships. Defeated by English fleet and storms.

questions.q: Which ancient city was famous for its Hanging Gardens, one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World?

source.notes: In modern-day Iraq. Their existence is debated by historians.

questions.q: Tenochtitlan was the capital of which pre-Columbian empire?

source.notes: Founded 1325. Now Mexico City. Conquered by Cortés in 1521.

questions.q: In the three accused women of the 1692 Salem witch trials, what crime were they charged with?

source.notes: Sarah Good, Sarah Osborne, and Tituba. 20 people were eventually executed.

questions.q: Which country was the first to put a human being in space?

source.notes: Yuri Gagarin, April 12, 1961. Vostok 1.

questions.q: Which Space Shuttle broke apart 73 seconds after launch on January 28, 1986?

source.notes: All 7 crew members died, including teacher Christa McAuliffe. Cause: O-ring failure.

questions.q: Which hurricane devastated New Orleans in August 2005?

source.notes: Category 5. Over 1,800 deaths. Levee failures flooded 80% of the city.

questions.q: The wave of anti-government protests that swept the Arab world beginning in 2010 is known as what?

source.notes: Started in Tunisia Dec 2010. Spread to Egypt, Libya, Syria, and others.

questions.q: 'Edo' was the historical name for which modern world capital?

source.notes: Renamed from Edo to Tokyo ('Eastern Capital') in 1868 during the Meiji Restoration.

questions.q: Which country hosted the first Eurovision Song Contest in 1956?

source.notes: Held in Lugano. 7 countries participated. Host winner was Switzerland.
